items.title: Default to adjacency list
items.avoid: Allocating a V×V matrix for a sparse graph just because matrices feel 'simpler'.
items.why: Real-world graphs (knowledge graphs, ML systems) are overwhelmingly sparse; lists save memory and traverse faster.
items.example:
```python
from collections import defaultdict
g: dict[str, list[tuple[str, int]]] = defaultdict(list)
```

items.title: Pick the algorithm that matches the edge weights
items.avoid: Using BFS on a weighted graph.
items.prefer: BFS for unweighted, Dijkstra for non-negative weights, Bellman-Ford for negative weights.
items.why: Each algorithm has a clear correctness precondition; mixing them produces silently wrong paths.
items.example:
```python
shortest = dijkstra(graph, src) if weighted else bfs(graph, src)
```

items.title: Detect cycles before topo-sorting
items.avoid: Assuming inputs are DAGs and silently returning a partial order on cyclic input.
items.prefer: Compare the number of emitted nodes to the total; raise if they differ.
items.why: A partial order causes downstream pipelines to skip tasks, often with no error.
items.example:
```python
if len(order) != len(nodes):
    raise ValueError('cycle detected')
```

items.title: Use a library when one will do
items.avoid: Re-implementing PageRank, community detection or shortest-path for the third time.
items.prefer: `networkx` for general graphs, `igraph` for very large ones, `graphblas` / `networkit` for performance.
items.why: These are well-tested and benefit from C-level acceleration.
items.example:
```python
import networkx as nx
G = nx.DiGraph()
G.add_weighted_edges_from([('A','B',4),('A','C',1)])
nx.dijkstra_path(G, 'A', 'C')
```

items.title: Iterate, don't recurse, on big graphs
items.avoid: Recursive DFS on graphs you don't control.
items.prefer: Iterative DFS with an explicit stack.
items.why: Python's recursion limit is ~1000; deep traversals raise RecursionError.
items.example:
```python
def dfs(g, src):
    seen, stack = set(), [src]
    while stack:
        n = stack.pop()
        if n in seen: continue
        seen.add(n)
        stack.extend(nb for nb, *_ in g.get(n, []) if nb not in seen)
```
